text: Welcome to another episode of inguinal hernia videos. Today we're going to be reviewing the microfacial inguinal anatomy that we've all become much more familiar with and much more enthusiastic about as a result of key leaders like Dr. David Laurier giving his spectacular lectures. This is a case of a sixty-five-year-old presenting with a left groin bulge. Her surgical history was notable for some pelvic operations, physical exam notable for a reducible hernia, and an ultrasound that showed an indirect hernia and possible femoral hernia.

So today we're going to talk about the microfascial dissection planes and review the way that I approach these during MIS inguinal. Medial dissection for me is a little different than what's been described by others. I like to actually start my dissection in the visceral compartment and then jump into the parietal compartment when I'm definitively over the bladder or in the retropubic space. My lateral dissection, I do like to maintain a visceral compartment throughout. So let's start looking at the video.

The surgeon begins the peritoneal dissection at the medial umbilical ligament using energy, carefully developing the pre-peritoneal space while avoiding the pre-transverse salus plane. The video shows the surgeon maintaining the intermediate fascia against the rectus to prevent bleeding. The green highlights indicate the visceral compartment, with the surgeon transitioning from the parietal to the visceral compartment during the lateral dissection. The areolar tissue planes are visible as the surgeon demonstrates the visceral compartment.

The surgeon incises the intermediate fascia to access the lateral visceral compartment, reflecting the fascia and areolar tissue towards the sidewall to protect nerves and vasculature. The peritoneum is isolated in the surgeon's hand, and fatty and areolar tissues are reflected. A small indirect hernia sac is reduced, and the surgeon begins developing the critical view of the myopectineal orifice.

The surgeon incises the intermediate fascia over the bladder, entering the parietal compartment. The green highlights indicate the parietal compartment as the fascia is reflected down towards the bladder for protection. The dissection continues in the retropubic area, identifying potential hernia sites, including a small preperitoneal fat femoral hernia, which is reduced. The surgeon then works to expose the lacunar ligament.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection, reflecting tissue towards the sidewall, and proceeds to identify the round ligament in preparation for transection.

Some recent publications have even highlighted that transection of the round ligament during inguinal hernia repair is associated with appropriate outcomes. It really serves no functional role. And by taking it, you are assuring that the mesh will lay nicely against the myopic tinea orifice. Some of the nuances, making sure that you take the round ligament with energy as it can sometimes bleed, and then taking it close to the peritoneum rather than close to the deep ring to avoid any injuries to the genital branch of the general femoral nerve. Now, sort of tidying up our final dissection. making sure that we cross the midline. This patient did not have a large medial hernia, and so I did not feel it was necessary to view the contralateral Cooper's ligament. Now tidying up our dissection, making sure that our mesh will have a great landing zone. And at this point, I'm pretty satisfied with our critical view. clearly identified the iliopubic tract, rolled out a cord lipoma, and had a good light dissection, making it ready for mesh positioning.

The surgeon places a mid-weight polypropylene mesh in the myopectineal orifice (MPO) with extensive coverage. The mesh is fixated with two points: one stitch through the Cooper's ligament and another through the lacunar ligament. The flap is closed using a standard technique, with insufflation pressure reduced to 6-8 mmHg, closing from lateral to medial.

Case went really well. No issues intraoperatively, of course, and then postoperatively, patient did very well, was discharged and required no narcotics in their post-op period. So there you have it, a review of the microfascial anatomy and how I utilize the intermediate fascia and the various dissection planes during MIS and moral hernia. Thank you very much.
